_meta.authoringNotes: Pathophysiology and classification per Cicardi et al. Allergy 2014 (PMID 24673465). ACE-inhibitor angioedema risk per Toh et al. Arch Intern Med 2012 (PMID 22732751). Treatment of acute hereditary angioedema attacks per Maurer et al. Allergy 2022 (PMID 35001423) WAO/EAACI guideline. Histaminergic angioedema overlaps with chronic urticaria — Zuberbier et al. Allergy 2022 EAACI/GA²LEN guideline (PMID 34536239).

h1Title: Angioedema (Allergic): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Angioedema is sudden, asymmetric, non-pitting swelling of the deeper dermis, subcutaneous tissue, or submucosa, most often involving the lips, eyelids, tongue, larynx, hands, feet, or genitals. Around 15-25% of people develop at least one episode in their lifetime, and roughly half of acute episodes seen in emergency departments are allergic (histaminergic) in origin, triggered by foods, drugs, latex, or insect stings. Swelling typically appears within 30 minutes to two hours of exposure, lasts 24-72 hours, and resolves without scarring. Laryngeal involvement is the immediate danger — it can asphyxiate within minutes. Effective rescue treatment exists with intramuscular adrenaline, antihistamines, and corticosteroids; bradykinin-mediated forms (hereditary, ACE-inhibitor-related) need different drugs entirely.

keyStats.prevalence: Lifetime prevalence of any angioedema episode 15-25% of the population; chronic urticaria with angioedema affects roughly 0.5-1% of adults
keyStats.demographics: Histaminergic angioedema affects women 2x more than men; ACE-inhibitor angioedema is 3-5x more common in Black patients than White
keyStats.avgAge: Allergic angioedema peaks in young adults aged 20-40; ACE-inhibitor angioedema peaks in adults aged 50-70
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 80,000-110,000 US emergency department visits annually for angioedema (NEDS data)

definition: Angioedema (ICD-10: T78.3 for allergic; D84.1 for hereditary C1 inhibitor deficiency) is localized, transient, well-demarcated swelling of the deep dermis and subcutaneous or submucosal tissue caused by plasma leakage from postcapillary venules. Two distinct molecular pathways drive the syndrome. Histamine-mediated (mast cell) angioedema, the focus of this page, accompanies most acute allergic reactions and chronic urticaria, develops within minutes to two hours, responds to antihistamines, corticosteroids, and adrenaline, and is frequently itchy and accompanied by hives. Bradykinin-mediated angioedema includes hereditary angioedema (HAE) from C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency, acquired C1-INH deficiency, and ACE-inhibitor-induced angioedema; these forms develop over hours, last longer, do not itch, do not respond to adrenaline or antihistamines, and need bradykinin pathway blockers or plasma-derived C1-INH. The condition is managed by allergists/immunologists, emergency physicians, and primary care doctors; recurrent or refractory cases need specialty referral. Allergic angioedema may occur alone or as part of full anaphylaxis with hypotension, bronchospasm, and gastrointestinal symptoms, in which case it constitutes a medical emergency requiring intramuscular adrenaline.

typesClassification.description: Triggered by foods (peanut, tree nuts, shellfish, egg, milk, sesame), drugs (beta-lactam antibiotics, NSAIDs), latex, or hymenoptera (bee, wasp) stings. Onset within minutes to two hours, often accompanied by urticaria, pruritus, flushing, and sometimes systemic anaphylaxis.

Recurrent angioedema with or without urticaria where no allergen is identified despite testing. Considered a variant of chronic spontaneous urticaria when accompanied by hives. Responds to high-dose antihistamines and omalizumab.
NSAIDs, opioids, vancomycin, and radiocontrast media trigger mast cell degranulation without IgE involvement. Clinical features overlap with IgE-mediated reactions and treatment is the same.
Inducible physical urticaria variants in which mechanical pressure, vibration, or shear stress trigger deep tissue swelling. Symptoms appear 4-8 hours after the trigger and last 12-24 hours.
Rare cyclical syndrome of recurrent angioedema, weight gain, fever, and marked peripheral blood eosinophilia (often >5,000/µL). Responds to corticosteroids.

primarySymptoms: Rapid, non-pitting, asymmetric swelling of the lips, eyelids, tongue, cheeks, or genitals that develops over minutes to two hours and resolves over 24-72 hours.
Itching, burning, or tingling at the swelling site (typical of histaminergic forms; bradykinin-mediated angioedema is non-itchy).
Urticarial wheals (hives) on the trunk, arms, or legs accompanying the deeper swelling in roughly 50% of allergic episodes.
Throat tightness, hoarseness, drooling, or stridor from laryngeal involvement — a life-threatening sign requiring immediate adrenaline.
Abdominal pain, cramps, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea from bowel-wall edema, more common in bradykinin-mediated forms but seen in severe allergic episodes too.
Tongue swelling that distorts speech and may obstruct the airway within 10-30 minutes.
Eye swelling (periorbital edema) closing one or both eyes shut, usually unilateral or asymmetric.
Painful, tense swelling of the hands, feet, or scrotum that does not pit on pressure.
Flushing, warmth, and a sensation of impending doom when angioedema is part of systemic anaphylaxis.
Hypotension, tachycardia, dizziness, or collapse if angioedema progresses to full anaphylactic shock.
earlyWarningSigns: Tingling or itching of the lips, tongue, or palate within minutes of eating a suspected food or taking a new medication
Hives appearing on the trunk shortly before deeper swelling develops
A scratchy throat or change in voice that precedes more obvious swelling
Crampy abdominal pain with no obvious cause in a person with prior episodes
Recurrent unexplained lip or eyelid swelling lasting one to three days
emergencySigns: Tongue or throat swelling with hoarseness, stridor, or difficulty swallowing saliva — give intramuscular adrenaline immediately and call emergency services
Difficulty breathing, audible wheezing, or chest tightness suggesting anaphylactic bronchospasm
Loss of consciousness, weak rapid pulse, pale clammy skin — signs of anaphylactic shock
Severe abdominal pain with vomiting that mimics a surgical abdomen, especially in a patient with known angioedema
Angioedema occurring on an ACE inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker that does not respond to adrenaline within 10 minutes — needs icatibant or fresh-frozen plasma

causes.description: Cross-linking of IgE on mast cells by food antigens (peanut, tree nuts, fish, shellfish, milk, egg, wheat, soy, sesame) releases histamine, tryptase, and leukotrienes. Onset within 30 minutes; reactions often involve lip and tongue first, then progress to urticaria and possible anaphylaxis.

Beta-lactam antibiotics, sulfonamides, and some chemotherapy agents trigger IgE-mediated angioedema. NSAIDs (aspirin, ibuprofen) and opioids cause non-IgE pseudo-allergic mast cell degranulation. Both pathways present clinically the same and respond to antihistamines, steroids, and adrenaline when severe.

Bee, wasp, hornet, yellow jacket, and fire ant stings deliver venom proteins recognized by IgE in sensitized individuals. Local large reactions are common; systemic angioedema and anaphylaxis occur in 1-3% of stings in unsensitized people but rise dramatically once sensitization is established.

Natural rubber latex proteins cross-react with banana, avocado, kiwi, and chestnut allergens. Healthcare workers, patients with spina bifida, and those with multiple prior surgeries are at highest risk. Reactions appear within minutes of mucosal or wound contact.

In roughly 40% of chronic angioedema-with-urticaria cases no allergen is found. Underlying mast cell activation may involve autoimmune IgG against FcɛRI or IgE, low-grade infection, hormonal triggers, or true mast cell disorders (mastocytosis). Workup includes serum tryptase and screening for autoantibodies.

Cold, heat, sunlight, pressure, vibration, and exercise can induce localized mast cell degranulation in susceptible individuals. Exercise-induced angioedema may be wheat-dependent (WDEIA) and is often missed unless the food-exercise combination is reproduced.

riskFactors.factor: Personal or family history of atopy
riskFactors.description: Asthma, allergic rhinitis, atopic dermatitis, and food allergies all share an underlying Th2-skewed immune profile. Atopic individuals carry roughly 2-3x higher risk of allergic angioedema episodes.

riskFactors.factor: Prior allergic reaction to the same trigger
riskFactors.description: Sensitization to a food, drug, or venom dramatically increases the severity of future reactions. The second exposure is typically faster and more systemic than the first.
Histaminergic and idiopathic angioedema are 2x more common in adult women than men, particularly between ages 20 and 50, partly reflecting hormonal modulation of mast cell reactivity.
Aspirin and other COX-1 inhibitors cause pseudo-allergic angioedema in 0.3-0.9% of adults and can worsen pre-existing urticaria. Selective COX-2 inhibitors are usually better tolerated.

Patients with a documented allergy to one antibiotic class have approximately a 10% chance of being labeled allergic to additional drug classes, though true cross-reactivity is often overestimated and warrants formal testing.
Healthcare workers, dental staff, food handlers, and rubber industry workers face cumulative latex exposure that drives sensitization in 4-17% over a career.
Hereditary alpha-tryptasemia and systemic mastocytosis lower the threshold for clinically significant mast cell degranulation, leading to recurrent angioedema and idiopathic anaphylaxis.

affectedDemographics: Lifetime prevalence of any angioedema episode roughly 15-25% in Western populations (Cicardi et al. Allergy 2014).
Acute angioedema accounts for 0.1-0.5% of all US emergency department visits (Banerji et al. Ann Allergy Asthma Immunol 2008).
Histaminergic angioedema with concurrent urticaria affects 0.5-1% of adults at any given time.
ACE-inhibitor-induced angioedema occurs in approximately 0.1-0.7% of patients on those drugs and 3-5x more frequently in Black than White patients.
Severe pediatric food-triggered angioedema is most common in children aged 1-5 with cow's milk, egg, and peanut as the leading triggers.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is clinical. The history establishes timing (minutes to hours), associated urticaria or itching (supports histaminergic), accompanying anaphylactic features (hypotension, bronchospasm), recent drug or food exposures, and family history (raises suspicion for hereditary angioedema). Physical examination during an attack confirms non-pitting, asymmetric, well-demarcated swelling without erythema in bradykinin forms or with surrounding flush and wheals in histaminergic forms. Laboratory tests confirm the mechanism: serum tryptase drawn 30 minutes to 4 hours after an anaphylactic episode rises in true mast-cell-driven reactions; complement studies (C4, C1-INH antigen and function, C1q) distinguish hereditary and acquired bradykinin-mediated angioedema from histaminergic forms. Specific IgE testing (ImmunoCAP) and skin prick testing identify likely allergic triggers; oral food challenge under supervision remains the diagnostic standard when history is unclear. Recurrent isolated angioedema without urticaria and not responding to antihistamines must be assumed bradykinin-mediated until proven otherwise — testing C4 (low between attacks in HAE), C1-INH antigen, and C1-INH function is mandatory. Drug-induced angioedema is identified by stopping the offending agent and watching for resolution over weeks. Imaging is rarely needed except in suspected bowel-wall angioedema, where CT shows segmental wall thickening and ascites.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies trigger food, drug, sting, or latex exposure and clarifies timing, severity, and prior episodes
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minute consultation with an allergist or immunologist. Bring a written list of foods, drugs, supplements, and any photographs of swelling. A symptom diary covering meals, exercise, and medications for two weeks before the episode is especially useful.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum total IgE and specific IgE (ImmunoCAP)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms IgE-mediated sensitization to a suspected food, latex, or venom antigen
diagnosticTests.whatToExpected: Standard blood draw. Results return in 5-10 days. Sensitivity 70-90% for established food allergens; positive result must be interpreted with clinical history because asymptomatic sensitization is common.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results return in 5-10 days. Sensitivity 70-90% for established food allergens; a positive result must be interpreted with clinical history because asymptomatic sensitization is common.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects IgE-mediated sensitization to inhalant, food, venom, or drug allergens at the bedside
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Drops of standardized allergen extracts are placed on the forearm and pricked through. A wheal at 15-20 minutes indicates sensitization. Antihistamines must be stopped 5-7 days beforehand. Mild itching and small wheals are expected.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rises within 30 minutes to 4 hours of mast-cell-mediated reactions; persistently elevated baseline suggests mastocytosis or hereditary alpha-tryptasemia
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood drawn during or shortly after the episode and again at least 24 hours later when symptoms have settled. Result available within 3-5 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Complement panel (C4, C1-INH antigen, C1-INH function, C1q)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes hereditary and acquired bradykinin-mediated angioedema from histaminergic forms; mandatory in any patient with recurrent angioedema without urticaria
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results take 7-14 days. C4 is low between attacks in 95% of HAE patients; C1-INH antigen and/or function below normal in type I and II HAE. C1q is low only in acquired forms.

diagnosticTests.test: Oral food challenge under medical supervision
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms or excludes clinically significant food allergy when history and IgE testing are equivocal
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Gradual increasing doses of the suspected food given over 2-4 hours in a clinic equipped to treat anaphylaxis. Vital signs and symptoms are monitored continuously. Plan for a full half-day visit.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms or excludes a true drug allergy when history is inconclusive and the drug is needed for future treatment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed in a monitored setting only. Single graded doses of the suspect drug are given over hours under physician supervision with resuscitation equipment available. Usually requires hospital day-case admission.

treatmentOverview: Acute allergic angioedema is treated by reversing mast cell mediator effects, securing the airway, and removing the trigger. For any angioedema involving the tongue, larynx, or accompanied by hypotension or bronchospasm, intramuscular adrenaline 0.3-0.5 mg into the anterolateral thigh is the first action and may be repeated every 5-15 minutes; delay in adrenaline is the single biggest avoidable cause of death in anaphylaxis. Adjuncts include H1 antihistamines (cetirizine 10 mg or diphenhydramine 50 mg orally; chlorphenamine 10 mg intravenously), H2 antihistamines (ranitidine, famotidine), and intravenous corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 60-125 mg or hydrocortisone 100-200 mg) which act over 4-6 hours and limit late-phase recurrence. Nebulized adrenaline can buy time for laryngeal swelling. If the patient is on an ACE inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker the drug must be stopped immediately and never restarted. Chronic and recurrent histaminergic angioedema is managed with non-sedating H1 antihistamines (cetirizine, fexofenadine, bilastine) at standard dose, escalating up to four times the licensed dose per the EAACI 2022 chronic urticaria guideline; omalizumab 300 mg subcutaneously every four weeks is added for antihistamine-refractory disease and provides complete control in roughly 65-70% of patients. Identified triggers are strictly avoided and patients are equipped with self-injectable adrenaline pens (two doses, recheck expiry), an emergency action plan, and medical-alert identification. Venom immunotherapy is curative for hymenoptera allergy in over 90% of treated adults.

medicalTreatments.name: Intramuscular adrenaline (epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg adult, 0.01 mg/kg pediatric)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for any angioedema with airway involvement or systemic anaphylactic features. Given into the anterolateral thigh; repeatable every 5-15 minutes. Self-injectors (EpiPen, Jext, Anapen) are prescribed for at-risk patients in pairs.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Single intramuscular dose resolves anaphylactic symptoms in 70-80% of cases; early administration reduces fatal outcomes by an order of magnitude

medicalTreatments.name: Non-sedating H1 antihistamines (cetirizine 10 mg, fexofenadine 180 mg, bilastine 20 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Mainstay of histaminergic angioedema control. The 2022 EAACI guideline supports up-dosing to 4x the licensed dose in patients not controlled at standard dose before adding other therapies.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete symptom control at standard dose in 40-50% of chronic urticaria with angioedema; up to 70% at 4x dose

medicalTreatments.name: Systemic corticosteroids (prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg orally 3-5 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Used as adjunct in severe acute episodes to dampen late-phase reactions over 4-12 hours. Not first-line in chronic management because of side-effect burden. Short courses are preferred over chronic dosing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces late-phase recurrence after biphasic anaphylaxis from 20% to under 5% in some series

medicalTreatments.name: Omalizumab (anti-IgE) 300 mg subcutaneous every 4 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Indicated for chronic spontaneous urticaria with angioedema unresponsive to high-dose antihistamines. Binds free IgE, reduces FcɛRI surface expression on mast cells and basophils.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete response in 35-44%, partial response in another 25-30%; benefit usually evident within 12 weeks

medicalTreatments.name: Cyclosporine 3-5 mg/kg/day
medicalTreatments.description: Calcineurin inhibitor used as off-label third-line treatment for antihistamine- and omalizumab-refractory disease. Requires blood pressure and renal function monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom control in approximately 65% over 6-12 weeks; relapse common on stopping

medicalTreatments.name: Venom immunotherapy
medicalTreatments.description: Subcutaneous build-up and 3-5 year maintenance for confirmed hymenoptera venom anaphylaxis. Reduces risk of systemic reaction on re-sting from 30-60% pre-treatment to under 3% post-treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Protective in 95-98% of treated adults against bee and 96% against vespid stings during and after the 3-5 year course

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency surgical airway (cricothyroidotomy)
surgicalOptions.description: Performed when laryngeal angioedema obstructs the airway and intubation fails. Bridges the patient to a definitive surgical or percutaneous tracheostomy in operating room conditions.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful airway establishment in 90-95% of attempts by trained personnel within 60 seconds

surgicalOptions.name: Awake fiberoptic intubation
surgicalOptions.description: Preferred method of airway control in progressive laryngeal angioedema when intubation is still possible. Performed by an anesthesiologist with topical anesthesia and minimal sedation to preserve airway tone.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful first-pass intubation in 85-90% of cases in experienced hands

alternativeTreatments.name: Allergen avoidance with dietitian-led elimination diet
alternativeTreatments.description: For confirmed food-triggered angioedema. Structured plans cut accidental exposure rates and address nutritional adequacy, especially in children avoiding cow's milk, egg, or wheat.

alternativeTreatments.name: Vitamin D repletion
alternativeTreatments.description: Observational studies link 25-OH vitamin D deficiency with poorer chronic urticaria/angioedema control. Repletion to a 25-OH level above 30 ng/mL is reasonable in deficient patients, though randomized evidence is limited.

whySeeSpecialist: Allergy and immunology referral is recommended after any episode of angioedema with airway involvement, systemic anaphylactic features, suspected drug allergy that limits future treatment options, or three or more attacks per year without an identified trigger. A specialist confirms the mechanism, performs skin and challenge testing safely, initiates omalizumab or venom immunotherapy, and creates a written emergency plan.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a board-certified allergist/immunologist with hospital affiliation for inpatient management of refractory or severe cases. Centers offering food and drug challenge testing, venom immunotherapy, and access to biologics such as omalizumab are best for recurrent or complex angioedema. Verify experience with both histaminergic and bradykinin-mediated forms.
hospitalCriteria: On-site emergency department equipped to manage anaphylaxis 24/7
Access to intramuscular and intravenous adrenaline, antihistamines, and corticosteroids
Difficult airway team with fiberoptic intubation capability
Specialist allergy/immunology outpatient clinic offering skin testing and challenge protocols
Pharmacy stocking C1-inhibitor concentrate or icatibant for differential diagnosis
keyFacilities: Emergency departments with critical care backup
preventionStrategies: Strictly avoid foods, drugs, latex, or insects identified as the cause; read every food label and discuss every new medication with the prescriber.
Carry two in-date adrenaline auto-injectors at all times and replace them by the expiry date.
Wear a medical-alert bracelet listing the trigger and angioedema diagnosis.
Inform school, work, restaurant staff, and family members of the trigger and the emergency action plan.
Document the reaction in the patient's electronic health record allergy section and flag relevant medication classes.
Receive venom immunotherapy after a systemic reaction to bee, wasp, hornet, or fire-ant sting to prevent future life-threatening episodes.
lifestyleModifications: Pre-medicate before any planned exposure that historically triggered mild reactions (e.g. premedication for radiocontrast in patients with prior reactions).
Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin if they have previously worsened urticaria or angioedema; use paracetamol or selective COX-2 inhibitors after discussion with a doctor.
Keep a symptom and exposure diary covering food, drugs, exercise, stress, and menstruation to detect patterns.
Limit alcohol, hot showers, and overheating during active flare periods because they can lower the threshold for mast cell activation.
Maintain a sleep schedule and stress reduction routine; chronic urticaria flares cluster around poor sleep and emotional stress.
Update the emergency action plan and review auto-injector technique annually with a clinician or pharmacist.

recommended: Whole, single-ingredient foods that simplify label reading and reduce hidden allergen exposure
Calcium- and vitamin-D-rich alternatives when avoiding cow's milk (fortified plant milks, fortified cereals, leafy greens)
Omega-3-rich fish (salmon, mackerel) where fish allergy is not the trigger — anti-inflammatory effects support skin barrier
Adequate hydration (2-2.5 L of water daily) during flares
avoid: Any documented food trigger, including small amounts in shared kitchens and processed foods
Pseudo-allergen-rich foods (food colorings, preservatives, salicylate-rich foods) only if a personal trigger has been identified through diary review
Alcohol, particularly red wine and beer with high histamine content, during active chronic urticaria flares
Aged cheeses, fermented foods, and cured meats only if personally triggering

exerciseGuidelines: Light to moderate aerobic exercise is safe between episodes and reduces stress-related flares. Patients with exercise-induced angioedema should avoid exercise within 2-4 hours of eating known co-triggers (especially wheat in WDEIA), carry an auto-injector when training, and exercise with a companion who knows the emergency plan.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed antihistamines at the same time each day, even when symptom-free, during active management periods.
Carry two auto-injectors and check expiry dates monthly; many smartphone reminders are available.
Wear a medical-alert ID and store an emergency action plan on the lock screen of the phone.
Photograph any new swelling with a date stamp to share with the clinician at follow-up.
Review meal and medication labels before consumption when allergens are confirmed.
Attend follow-up appointments at 3, 6, and 12 months after diagnosis to titrate therapy.
prognosis: Allergic angioedema with an identified trigger has an excellent prognosis when avoidance is achievable: future episodes are largely preventable, and the standby auto-injector resolves accidental exposures in 70-80% with a single dose. Recurrent histaminergic angioedema with chronic urticaria is benign but persistent, with median duration of 1-5 years; spontaneous remission occurs in roughly 30-50% of patients within five years, and modern stepwise therapy controls symptoms in over 80% of treated individuals. Venom immunotherapy delivers durable protection in 95-98% of hymenoptera-allergic adults. Mortality from allergic angioedema is overwhelmingly driven by airway compromise: case-fatality is under 1% when adrenaline is given promptly and rises sharply when adrenaline is delayed or omitted. The most predictive risk factor for fatal outcome is asthma — uncontrolled asthma triples the risk of fatal anaphylaxis in food-triggered reactions and warrants intensified maintenance therapy.
recoveryTimeline: Acute episodes peak within 30 minutes to 2 hours and resolve over 24-72 hours with treatment. Hives accompanying the swelling clear within 6-24 hours. Bowel-wall swelling can take 48-72 hours to settle. Steroid-treated late-phase reactions resolve over 4-12 hours. Chronic forms typically respond to optimized therapy within 4-12 weeks; omalizumab benefit usually evident within 12 weeks.
complications: Laryngeal obstruction and asphyxiation — the leading fatal complication; rapid intramuscular adrenaline and surgical airway availability are essential.
Anaphylactic shock with hypotension, cardiac arrhythmia, and possible cardiac arrest in untreated systemic reactions.
Biphasic anaphylaxis with recurrence of symptoms 4-12 hours after initial resolution in 5-20% of severe episodes — observe in the emergency department for at least 6 hours.
Bowel-wall edema mimicking surgical abdomen and leading to unnecessary laparotomy if angioedema is not recognized.
Adrenaline-related complications (tachycardia, hypertension, tremor, transient cardiac ischemia in patients with coronary disease) — risk outweighed by benefit in true anaphylaxis.
Psychological burden including anxiety, food avoidance disorders, and reduced quality of life — addressable with structured education and dietitian support.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Allergy, Asthma & Immunology (AAAAI)
supportResources.description: Patient-facing condition guide, find-an-allergist tool, and printable action plans.

supportResources.name: Anaphylaxis UK
supportResources.description: Helpline, emergency action plan templates, and education for adults and children at risk of anaphylaxis.

supportResources.name: Food Allergy Research & Education (FARE)
supportResources.description: Food allergy patient organization with school plans, recipe resources, and clinical trial registry.

supportResources.name: European Academy of Allergy and Clinical Immunology
supportResources.description: Source of the 2022 EAACI/GA²LEN/EuroGuiDerm urticaria and angioedema clinical guideline.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hereditary Angioedema
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hereditary angioedema is bradykinin-mediated, develops slowly over hours, does not itch, has no hives, has a strong family history, and does not respond to adrenaline, antihistamines, or steroids. It needs C1 inhibitor concentrate, icatibant, or ecallantide. Always check C4 and C1-INH in recurrent angioedema without urticaria.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anaphylaxis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anaphylaxis is a systemic IgE-mediated reaction that may include angioedema along with bronchospasm, hypotension, and gastrointestinal symptoms. Angioedema is the visible swelling; anaphylaxis is the whole-body syndrome. All anaphylactic angioedema needs intramuscular adrenaline immediately.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Urticaria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic urticaria features superficial wheals (hives) lasting under 24 hours and often accompanies histaminergic angioedema. Up to 50% of chronic urticaria patients have angioedema episodes too. Treatment overlap is significant — both respond to antihistamines and omalizumab.

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Contact dermatitis is a delayed T-cell-mediated reaction producing eczematous, weeping, and intensely pruritic skin changes localized to areas of allergen contact (jewelry, cosmetics, plants). It develops over 24-72 hours rather than minutes and does not cause systemic angioedema.

relatedConditions.name: Anaphylaxis
relatedConditions.relevance: Systemic IgE-mediated reaction that frequently includes angioedema and shares treatment pathways with intramuscular adrenaline.

relatedConditions.name: Hereditary Angioedema
relatedConditions.relevance: Bradykinin-mediated swelling syndrome that is the most important non-allergic differential diagnosis for recurrent angioedema.

relatedConditions.name: Food Allergy
relatedConditions.relevance: Common trigger for IgE-mediated angioedema, particularly peanut, tree nuts, fish, shellfish, milk, egg, and sesame.

relatedConditions.name: Drug Allergy
relatedConditions.relevance: Antibiotics, NSAIDs, and contrast media frequently cause both immediate and delayed angioedema.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Urticaria
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic spontaneous urticaria and angioedema overlap clinically and share management with antihistamines and omalizumab.

faqs.question: What is angioedema in simple terms?
faqs.answer: Angioedema is sudden swelling of the deeper skin or mucous membranes, usually of the lips, eyes, tongue, hands, feet, or throat. Allergic angioedema is caused by mast cells releasing histamine and develops within minutes to two hours of exposure to a food, drug, latex, or insect sting. It usually clears within one to three days.

faqs.question: What are the most common triggers of allergic angioedema?
faqs.answer: Peanut, tree nuts, fish, shellfish, milk, egg, sesame, antibiotics (especially penicillins), NSAIDs, bee or wasp stings, and latex are the leading triggers. About 40% of recurrent allergic episodes have no identifiable trigger and are called idiopathic. A detailed food and drug diary often uncovers patterns missed during the visit.

faqs.question: How do I tell allergic angioedema from hereditary angioedema?
faqs.answer: Allergic angioedema develops quickly (minutes to two hours), often itches, often has hives, and responds to adrenaline, antihistamines, and steroids. Hereditary angioedema develops slowly over hours, does not itch, has no hives, runs in families, and needs C1 inhibitor concentrate or icatibant. C4 blood levels are low between attacks in hereditary cases.

faqs.question: When is angioedema a medical emergency?
faqs.answer: Any swelling involving the tongue, throat, or voice, any difficulty breathing or swallowing, hoarseness, drooling, dizziness, low blood pressure, or wheezing makes angioedema a medical emergency. Give intramuscular adrenaline immediately if available, call emergency services, and lie the person flat with legs raised unless they are vomiting.

faqs.question: How is allergic angioedema treated?
faqs.answer: Severe attacks need intramuscular adrenaline, oxygen, intravenous fluids, antihistamines, and corticosteroids. Milder attacks respond to oral antihistamines and a short course of steroids. Chronic recurrent attacks are managed with high-dose non-sedating antihistamines, omalizumab, and avoidance of confirmed triggers.

faqs.question: Can I use an EpiPen for angioedema?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the angioedema involves the throat, tongue, or breathing, or is accompanied by anaphylactic features like wheezing, low blood pressure, or vomiting. Inject into the outer thigh, hold for 3 seconds, and call emergency services. A second dose may be given after 5-15 minutes if symptoms persist.

faqs.question: How long does an episode of angioedema last?
faqs.answer: Allergic angioedema episodes peak within 30 minutes to two hours and resolve over 24-72 hours with treatment. Hives that accompany the swelling clear faster, within 6-24 hours. Bowel-wall swelling causing belly pain may take up to 72 hours to settle even on appropriate therapy.

faqs.question: Is angioedema dangerous?
faqs.answer: Angioedema involving only the lip, eye, or hand without airway or breathing involvement is uncomfortable but not life-threatening. Swelling of the tongue, throat, or larynx can asphyxiate within minutes. The most predictive risk factor for fatal anaphylaxis is uncontrolled asthma, which warrants aggressive asthma maintenance therapy.

faqs.question: What tests confirm allergic angioedema?
faqs.answer: Specific IgE blood tests (ImmunoCAP) and skin prick testing identify trigger foods, latex, and venoms. Serum tryptase drawn within 4 hours of the reaction confirms mast cell activation. Complement studies (C4, C1-INH antigen and function) rule out hereditary or acquired bradykinin-mediated forms. Oral food or drug challenges remain the gold standard when history and IgE are equivocal.

faqs.question: Can children outgrow angioedema?
faqs.answer: Many children outgrow milk, egg, wheat, and soy allergies by age 5-10, and the angioedema episodes triggered by these foods typically disappear too. Peanut, tree nut, fish, and shellfish allergies are usually lifelong. Regular reassessment by an allergist with serial specific-IgE and supervised food challenges guides re-introduction.

faqs.question: Is ACE inhibitor angioedema considered allergic?
faqs.answer: No. ACE inhibitor angioedema is bradykinin-mediated, not allergic, and does not respond to adrenaline, antihistamines, or steroids. The drug must be stopped permanently and the patient should never be re-challenged with any ACE inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker. Icatibant and fresh-frozen plasma have been used in severe cases.

faqs.question: Can stress cause angioedema?
faqs.answer: Stress does not directly cause angioedema but it lowers the threshold for mast cell activation in patients with chronic urticaria and angioedema. Flares often cluster around poor sleep, life events, and infections. Stress-reduction strategies, regular sleep, and treating underlying urticaria reduce the frequency of stress-related episodes.

faqs.question: Can angioedema affect the gut?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bowel-wall edema causes severe crampy abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and sometimes diarrhea. It is more common in bradykinin-mediated angioedema but can occur in severe allergic episodes too. CT shows segmental bowel wall thickening and ascites; the condition resolves within 24-72 hours with appropriate therapy.

faqs.question: How is angioedema prevented?
faqs.answer: Strict trigger avoidance is the cornerstone: read every food label, inform restaurants, and discuss every new medication with the prescriber. Carry two in-date adrenaline auto-injectors at all times. Wear a medical-alert bracelet and ensure family and coworkers know the action plan. Venom immunotherapy prevents future life-threatening reactions in confirmed sting allergy.

faqs.question: What is omalizumab and when is it used for angioedema?
faqs.answer: Omalizumab is a monoclonal antibody that binds free IgE and is given as a 300 mg subcutaneous injection every four weeks. It is used in chronic spontaneous urticaria with angioedema that is not controlled by high-dose antihistamines. Complete or partial response occurs in 60-70% of patients, usually within 12 weeks.

faqs.question: Can angioedema occur during exercise?
faqs.answer: Yes. Exercise-induced angioedema and anaphylaxis can develop within 30 minutes of vigorous activity. A subset is food-dependent, especially after wheat ingestion (wheat-dependent exercise-induced anaphylaxis, or WDEIA), where neither the food nor exercise alone causes symptoms but the combination does. Exercise with a companion and carry adrenaline.

faqs.question: Does pregnancy worsen angioedema?
faqs.answer: Most women with chronic urticaria and angioedema improve or remain stable during pregnancy because of natural increases in cortisol and progesterone-driven immune modulation. Antihistamines such as loratadine and cetirizine are considered low-risk in pregnancy. Severe attacks still warrant adrenaline; oxygenation of mother and fetus takes priority over theoretical drug risks.

faqs.question: Are over-the-counter antihistamines enough for angioedema?
faqs.answer: For mild localized swelling without airway or systemic symptoms, oral non-sedating antihistamines (cetirizine 10 mg, fexofenadine 180 mg, loratadine 10 mg) are often enough. Any angioedema involving the tongue, throat, breathing, or systemic features needs intramuscular adrenaline and emergency assessment, not antihistamines alone.

faqs.question: Can angioedema come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Biphasic anaphylaxis recurs in 5-20% of severe episodes within 4-12 hours of initial resolution, which is why emergency departments observe patients for at least 6 hours after a major reaction. Chronic recurrent angioedema can flare unpredictably; staying on maintenance antihistamines and avoiding triggers reduce recurrence.

faqs.question: How does angioedema differ from regular swelling after an injury?
faqs.answer: Angioedema is non-pitting, asymmetric, and develops within minutes to hours without obvious trauma. Injury-related swelling is pitting (leaves a dent under pressure), pinkish or bruised, painful, and follows a clear mechanism. Allergic angioedema also responds to antihistamines and adrenaline, which have no effect on traumatic edema.

faqs.question: Should I avoid all medications after one drug-allergic angioedema?
faqs.answer: No. The specific drug and structurally similar agents should be avoided, but alternatives within the same broad class are often safe. A formal drug allergy evaluation with skin testing or supervised challenge clarifies which drugs are safe to re-use. Many patients labeled penicillin-allergic are not truly allergic on retesting.

metaDescription: Allergic angioedema: sudden lip, tongue, or throat swelling from food, drugs, or stings. Triggers, emergency care, antihistamine and adrenaline treatment.
